title: Whole-Genome Sequencing and Comparative Genomic Analysis of Citrobacter farmeri and Enterobacter cloacae from Unhatched Green Turtle Eggs.
authors: Nurcan Önen, Bahadır Törün, Can Yılmaz
summary: Scientists studied bacteria found in dead sea turtle eggs on a Mediterranean beach and decoded the complete genetic instructions of two harmful bacterial species. They discovered these bacteria have genes that make them resistant to antibiotics and tools to infect and damage the turtle embryos.
key_points: Two types of bacteria (Citrobacter farmeri and Enterobacter cloacae) were found in unhatched green turtle eggs at a nesting beach
The bacteria carry multiple antibiotic-resistance genes, making them harder to treat with common medicines
The bacteria possess virulence factors that help them stick to cells and steal iron, enabling them to cause infections
The research suggests monitoring bacterial threats could help protect endangered sea turtle populations
why_it_matters: Understanding what bacteria are killing sea turtle eggs and how resistant they are to treatment is crucial for conserving endangered sea turtles and managing nesting beaches effectively.
